reasoning: Wallis and Futuna is a peaceful French territory with a population of only about 11,000 people. It has no continuous standing army or multiple organized armed groups. The ACLED dataset defines "Battles" as violent interactions between two organized armed groups. Given the most recent known baseline value corresponds exactly to an average of 0 Battles over the past 360 days, checking if there will be "more than ten times as many 'Battles'" mathematically means requiring strictly more than 10 Battles (so 11 or more) in a single 30-day window. Eleven battles in a 30-day period would imply a major civil war or sustained and highly active armed conflict, occurring at a rate of one battle every 2-3 days. There is absolutely no news, evidence, or historical precedent to suggest any localized war or armed group proliferation in Wallis and Futuna. Furthermore, protests and riots do not count as "Battles" under the ACLED methodology (they are separate event types), making the 11-battle threshold even harder to hit. Thus, the probability of reaching >=11 Battles for any of the 30-day periods is basically zero. I have set my probabilities to the minimum allowable bound (0.05) and slightly increased the probabilities for further-out dates to reflect any negligible but incrementally increasing tail risk over time.
